books.title: Everyone Else Seems So Chill
books.subtitle: For when everyone online looks effortlessly available and your own need for a door and a quiet hour starts to feel like a defect
books.desc: Everyone else seems fine being on call all the time. They get added to the group, the project, the plans, and they just say yes — no flinch, no dread, no whole internal negotiation. Meanwhile you need a door, a quiet hour, a 'no,' and it makes you feel high-maintenance for existing. This is for the student who keeps losing a contest they never agreed to enter.
books.long_desc:
Scroll for thirty seconds and the evidence is everywhere. Everyone else is so chill. They reply instantly and it costs them nothing. They get pulled into the group chat, the late-night plans, the favor, the project, and they just say yeah, easy, no drama. They seem to need nothing — no quiet hour, no door, no time to recover from being around people. And then there's you, who needs all of it, and the wanting starts to feel like a defect everyone else got vaccinated against.

So you measure. Against the friend who's always down. Against the one who never seems drained. Against the whole timeline of people who look like being available is effortless. And every time, you come up short — not because you did anything wrong, but because you pointed a very precise instrument at the wrong target: yourself, against an audience that's also performing fine.

This book is for you. The one who's quietly decided that needing limits makes you more difficult, more sensitive, more high-maintenance than everyone else seems to be. The one running a comparison you never consented to, against a version of other people that doesn't actually exist — because you're seeing their highlight reel of availability and your own unedited need.

Here you'll come to recognize the contest for what it is, and how it feeds itself — every 'they're so chill' a fresh way to come up short. You'll see that the people who look like they need nothing are mostly just better at hiding the cost, or paying it somewhere you can't see. You'll feel the difference between an actual limit and the story that your limit makes you worse than the room. You'll find the door is yours to close on your own schedule, not everyone else's — and that the question isn't how little can you get away with needing, but where, honestly, do you draw the line. Yours. Not theirs.

Nothing here will help you win the comparison. That's the point. There's no tier of chill you can finally reach where needing space stops counting against you, because the contest was rigged from the start. This book won't optimize you into someone lower-maintenance, won't teach you to need less, won't hand you a way to out-chill the timeline. It will just take you out of the race.

You were never behind. You were running a comparison that has no finish line, against people who are also tired. You're allowed to want what you want at the volume you want it.

Book 3 of the Present Tense Books boundaries series.
books.angle: They reply at 2am like it's nothing. You read receipt and feel the weight of it.
books.author: Talia Petrova
keywords: feeling high maintenance for having needs
everyone else seems fine but me
social comparison and self worth young adults
boundaries when you feel too sensitive

books.title: The No That Feels Like Danger
books.subtitle: When your body sounds the alarm the second you want to say no — and how to tell a real threat from an old habit
books.desc: The text comes in and your chest tightens before you've even read it. Wanting to say no feels less like a preference and more like standing in a doorway you're not allowed to close. This is for the student whose nervous system treats a boundary like a threat, and who's tired of agreeing to everything just to make the alarm stop.
books.long_desc:
It happens before you can think. Someone asks for the thing — the favor, the yes, the 'you're coming, right?' — and your whole body goes tight. Heart faster. Stomach dropped. A low hum of dread you can't quite point to. You haven't even decided anything yet. But your body has already decided that wanting out of this is dangerous.

So you say yeah. For sure. No worries. And the alarm goes quiet, and you tell yourself that was easier, and you don't notice the cost until you're lying awake replaying a conversation that should have taken four seconds.

This book is for you — the one who is so used to being available that the idea of not being available reads as a five-alarm fire. You're not anxious for no reason. You learned, somewhere, that closing a door gets you in trouble. That a 'no' makes you the problem. That the safest move is to stay open, stay reachable, stay yes. Your body believed you, and now it sounds the alarm every time you so much as think about drawing a line.

Here you'll come to recognize the alarm for what it actually is. You'll feel the difference between a real threat and a body that's just bracing out of habit — the false alarm that fires in the seconds before you set a limit. You'll learn what's happening in your chest when the dread arrives, and that it can move through you without you having to obey it. You'll find the door is yours to close, and that closing it does not, in fact, end you. The threshold moment — the one where you decide where you draw the line — stops being a thing you flinch away from and becomes a thing you can stand in.

This book will not teach you to 'just say no' like it's nothing, because to you it isn't nothing. It won't hand you a script that makes you cold. It won't optimize your relationships or turn your limits into a flex. There's no version of you in here that needs less, feels less, or apologizes faster. The alarm isn't a flaw to delete. It's a smoke detector that got set too sensitive, and you're allowed to learn its real range.

You don't have to wait until the dread fades to be allowed to say no. You can feel the alarm and draw the line anyway. That's not danger. That's just you, finally on your own side.

Book 1 of the Present Tense Books boundaries series.
books.angle: Your body braced for impact before the 'no' even left your mouth.
books.author: Talia Petrova
keywords: how to set boundaries without feeling guilty
people pleasing and anxiety in teens
nervous system anxiety when setting limits
boundaries self help for young adults

books.title: It Makes Me Sound Difficult
books.subtitle: For when you've made yourself endlessly easy to be around because asking for anything feels like proof something's wrong with you
books.desc: You'd rather sit through the whole thing than be the person who needs it to be different. Somewhere along the way you decided that having limits makes you cold, dramatic, difficult, and so you became endlessly easy instead. This is for the student carrying a quiet belief that wanting space is proof there's something wrong with them.
books.long_desc:
You've gotten really good at being easy. Low-maintenance. Down for whatever. You read the room before you walk into it. You soften the text three times before you send it. You apologize for taking up space and then apologize for apologizing. And underneath all of it sits a belief you've never said out loud: that if you ever actually asked for what you needed — if you closed one door, said one real no — people would finally see the thing you've been covering for. That you're difficult. Too much. Hard to love.

This book is for you. Not the version you perform in the group chat. The one who learned, young, that a boundary made you the bad guy, so you stopped having them out loud and started managing everyone's comfort instead. The shame isn't loud. It's the quiet engine under all the agreeableness — the reason 'no' feels less like a sentence and more like a confession.

Here you'll come to recognize the belief for what it is: not a fact about you, but an old story you've been overworking to hide. You'll feel where it lives in your body, the flush of being seen as the demanding one. You'll start to tell the difference between being kind and disappearing. You'll learn that the people who only liked the frictionless, always-yes version of you were never actually in the room with the whole of you — and that the ones who stay when you draw a line are the only ones who were ever really there. The door you've been afraid to close turns out to be yours. The threshold you flinch from — where do you draw the line — becomes a place you're allowed to stand without being a worse person for it.

This book will not coach you into 'confidence' or hand you affirmations to repeat in the mirror. It won't tell you your boundaries are your superpower, because that's just the same pressure wearing a nicer outfit. It won't optimize you into someone more palatable. There is no fix in here, because you were never broken — you were just convinced that needing anything was a defect to manage.

Wanting space doesn't make you difficult. It makes you a person with an inside. You're allowed to have one. You're allowed to let it show.

Book 2 of the Present Tense Books boundaries series.
books.angle: You learned to be agreeable so no one would find the part you're hiding.
books.author: Talia Petrova
keywords: how to stop being a people pleaser young adult
fear of being seen as difficult

books.title: Everyone My Age Already Figured It Out
books.subtitle: For the student doing fine on paper who measures every day against a feed full of people who look like they were born sure of themselves
books.desc: You open the app and there it is again: someone your age who already knows what they're doing, already got in, already seems sure. You close it feeling like you're running late on a race nobody started. This book won't help you win the comparison — that's the whole point.
books.long_desc:
You open the app to escape the feeling, and the feeling is right there waiting for you.

Someone your age announcing the internship. Someone's little 'I can't believe this is my life' caption. Someone who, at seventeen, already moves through the world like they were handed the instructions you never got. You weren't even looking for it. You just wanted to not think for a second, and instead you're doing the math again — where you should be by now, how far behind you've fallen, how everyone else apparently got the memo.

This book is for you — the student who's actually doing okay, and can't feel it, because okay isn't the bar. The bar is everyone else, all at once, at their absolute best, on a screen built to show you nothing but their best. You're not lazy and you're not failing. You've just pointed a sharp, sensitive instrument at the wrong target: yourself, held up against a feed.

Comparison is its own doorway into imposter syndrome. It isn't the shame that hides or the spiral that loops or the alarm that fires for no reason. It's the ruler — the constant, quiet measuring that always, somehow, comes back with the same answer: not enough, not yet, not like them.

Chapter by chapter, you'll come to recognize the measuring as it's happening — the specific moment your stomach drops at a stranger's good news. You'll see what it costs to live as a number that's always lower than someone else's. You'll learn why the comparison is rigged: that you are seeing edited, posted, best-case fragments and weighing your whole unedited inside against them. You'll work with your body, in the room, phone face-down, in the actual moment the scroll turns on you — not through deleting the app forever, not through a gratitude list, not through becoming impressive enough to finally win.

This book will not help you come out ahead. It will not optimize your life so the comparison finally lands in your favor. It will not turn you into someone who posts the caption other people ache over. There's no fix here, because the comparison was never a problem to solve — it was a habit of pointing the instrument at yourself, and instruments can be set down.

Nothing here will help you win. That's the point. What it offers instead: you get to come back to your own life, the one happening off-screen, at its own pace, on no one's timeline but yours. Not measured. Not ranked. Not behind. Just here, where you actually are.

You're allowed to stop running a race nobody else is actually in.

Book 2 of the Present Tense Books imposter syndrome series.
books.angle: You're holding your behind-the-scenes up against everyone's highlight reel.
books.author: Mira Nolan
keywords: everyone else has it figured out

books.title: They'll Find Out I Faked It
books.subtitle: For the kid who looks like they have it together — and is quietly sure that if anyone saw the real them, the whole thing would fall apart
books.desc: You did the work, you got the grade, and you still feel like you snuck in the back door. This is the quiet belief that the competent version of you is a costume, and that underneath it there's something wrong that you have to keep covered. This book doesn't try to talk you out of it. It turns the light on slowly enough that you can finally look.
books.long_desc:
Someone praises your work and your whole body goes cold.

Not warm. Not proud. Cold — because now they expect that again, and you're certain you got it right by accident, and one day you'll turn in the real you and they'll see. You've gotten so good at the costume that almost no one knows there's a costume. You answer 'how'd you do that' with a shrug. You say 'oh it's not that good' before anyone else can. You overwork everything, not because you love it, but because the work is the only thing standing between you and the version of you you're sure is the truth.

This book is for you — the high schooler everyone thinks is fine. The one who got in, got picked, got the grade, and feels like a glitch in the system that hasn't been caught yet. The one carrying a quiet, specific belief: not 'I made a mistake' but 'something is wrong with me, and the work is how I keep it hidden.'

That belief has a name. It's shame, and it is the engine under imposter syndrome — different from the spiral that loops, different from the comparison that measures, different from the alarm that just fires. Shame is the part that's convinced the problem isn't what you did. It's what you are.

Chapter by chapter, you'll come to recognize the mask you built and what it actually costs to wear it all day. You'll notice the moment your body braces before someone can see you. You'll meet the hidden belief underneath — the one you've never said out loud — and you'll find out it isn't a fact about you. It's a thing you learned, in a feed that rewards the highlight reel and punishes the unfinished. You'll work with your body, in the room, with the phone face-down, in the actual moment the feeling shows up — not through worksheets, not through a morning routine, not through becoming someone more impressive.

This book will not make you confident. It will not optimize you. It will not hand you a script to fake it harder or a hack to feel worthy by Monday. There's no fix here, because you were never broken — you were hidden, and hidden isn't the same as broken.

What it offers instead: you get to stop performing a self you don't recognize. You get to be a real, unfinished person who is allowed to be seen. Not fixed. Not healed in some final, posted-about way. Just no longer alone with the thing you've been hiding.

You're allowed to put the costume down. Even just here. Even just for the length of this book.

Book 1 of the Present Tense Books imposter syndrome series.
books.angle: You're not hiding a mistake. You're hiding a person.
books.author: Mira Nolan

books.title: Your Life Looks Worse From Inside It
books.subtitle: For the student who opens the feed feeling fine and closes it a few inches smaller — because an ordinary, unposted Tuesday can't compete with everyone else's highlights
books.desc: Open the app and there they are: people exactly your age who already seem to have the friends, the skin, the plan, the unbothered calm. You close it feeling a few inches smaller than before. This book is for the student who measures their behind-the-scenes against everyone else's highlight reel and somehow always loses. It won't help you win the comparison. That's the whole point.
books.long_desc:
It's late, and you're scrolling. There's the person your age who already has the internship, the relationship, the apartment that looks like a mood board. There's the one who seems calm in a way you've never once felt. There's the one who's just... easy with themselves, like they got an instruction manual you didn't. You started the night feeling fine. Twenty minutes in, you feel like you're standing slightly behind your own life, watching everyone else's start without you.

This book is for you. The one who turned themselves into a measurement — who can't see a person their age without instantly running the math on who's ahead. You compare your messy inside to everyone else's clean outside and call the gap proof. You refresh to feel something and it only ever takes something. You're not vain and you're not failing. You've just aimed a very precise instrument at the worst possible target: yourself, mid-scroll, in the dark.

What you'll come to recognize is how the comparison actually works — and how rigged the game was from the first frame. You'll see the mask everyone's wearing, including the ones who look most arrived. You'll feel the exact cost of grading your whole worth on a curve set by a feed. You'll find the belief hiding underneath the scrolling — that you're behind, that everyone else got a head start, that there's a timeline and you're failing it — and you'll watch it come apart, because it was never true. There's no race. There's no curve. There was never a line you were behind.

Nothing in here will help you win. You won't out-post them, out-glow them, or finally pull ahead. That's the point. The comparison isn't a contest you're losing — it's a lens, and you can set it down.

And when you do set it down — when the phone goes face-down on the duvet and the feed stops feeding you other people — there's this quiet that rushes in. The room is still here. Your breath is still here. The night didn't go anywhere while you were grading yourself against strangers. It was just waiting for you to look up.

What this book offers instead is the strange quiet of looking up from the feed and finding that your actual life — the unfiltered, unposted, ordinary one happening in this exact room — was never behind anyone's. It was just yours. And it was always enough.

You can stop running someone else's numbers on yourself. You were never behind. You were just looking down.

Book 2 of the Present Tense Books self worth series.
books.angle: You're grading yourself against a feed that was never real.
books.author: Hannah Gowan
